metadata.title: English Conversation & Debate Questions by CEFR Level
metadata.description: A collection of 180 questions designed to elicit extended spoken responses, debate, and conversation. Difficulty, vocabulary, and topic abstraction are calibrated to each CEFR level.
metadata.framework: CEFR (Common European Framework of Reference for Languages)

A1.name: Beginner
A1.description: Simple personal questions using present simple, basic vocabulary, and familiar topics. Designed to elicit short narratives and personal preferences.

questions.question: What is your name and where are you from? Tell me about your hometown.
What is your favorite food? Why do you like it? How often do you eat it?
Do you have a big family? Tell me about the people in your family.
What do you usually do every morning before school or work?
What is your favorite color? Where do you see this color in your life?
Do you have a pet? Describe your pet, or describe a pet you would like to have.
What do you do in your free time? Tell me about your hobbies.
What is your favorite season of the year? What do you do in that season?
Do you like sports? Which sports do you watch or play?
What is your favorite day of the week? What do you usually do on that day?
Tell me about your best friend. What do you like to do together?
What kind of music do you like? Who is your favorite singer or band?
Do you like to cook? What food can you make? Who taught you?
Describe your bedroom. What do you have in it? What is your favorite thing in your bedroom?
What clothes do you like to wear? What do you wear to school, work, or a party?
Do you like your school or your job? Why or why not? Tell me about a normal day.
What is your favorite place in your city? Why do you like it?
What time do you go to bed and wake up? Do you sleep enough? Why or why not?
Do you like to travel? Where do you want to go in the future? Why?
What is your favorite movie or TV show? What is it about? Why do you like it?
How do you go to school or work every day? Do you like it? Why?
What do you usually eat for breakfast, lunch, and dinner? What is your favorite meal?
Do you prefer winter or summer? Tell me what you do in your favorite season.
What is your favorite holiday? How do you celebrate it with your family?
Tell me about your house or apartment. How many rooms does it have? What is your favorite room?
What languages can you speak? Why do you want to learn English?
What is your favorite subject in school? Why do you like it? Who is your favorite teacher?
Do you like reading? What kind of books do you read? Tell me about a book you like.
What fruits and vegetables do you like? What food don't you like? Why?
What makes you happy? What do you do when you feel sad?

A2.name: Elementary
A2.description: Questions using past tense, future forms, and comparatives. Topics include personal experiences, simple opinions, and basic descriptions of people, places, and events.

questions.question: Tell me about your last vacation. Where did you go and what did you do there?
What did you do last weekend? Did you have fun? What was the best part?
What are your plans for next year? What do you want to learn or change?
Have you ever traveled to another country or city? Tell me about that experience.
Compare your hometown with another city you know. Which one is better and why?
What was your favorite school subject when you were a child? Has it changed? Why?
Tell me about a person who is important to you. Why is this person special?
What is the best gift you have ever received? Who gave it to you and why?
Describe a typical day in your country. What do most people do in the morning, afternoon, and evening?
What was the last movie or show you watched? Did you enjoy it? Why or why not?
Have you ever tried unusual food? What was it like? Would you eat it again?
What did you want to be when you were a child? Has your dream changed? Why?
Compare life today with life 50 years ago. What is better now? What is worse?
Tell me about a memorable birthday. What did you do? Who was with you?
What is the best book or article you have ever read? What was it about?
What kind of weather do you prefer? Describe a perfect day for you.
Tell me about a special place you have visited. What was special about it?
What did you do to learn English? What was easy and what was difficult?
Describe your favorite restaurant. What food do they have? Why do you like going there?
What was the happiest day of your life? Why was it so special?
Do you prefer the city or the countryside? Why? Where would you like to live in the future?
Tell me about a time you helped someone. How did you feel after?
What did you usually do on weekends when you were a child? Has it changed?
Describe a friend from your childhood. Are you still friends? Why or why not?
What is more important: money or time? Why? How do you use yours?
Tell me about a sport or activity you tried for the first time. How was it?
What would you do if you had a free day with no responsibilities? Plan it for me.
Compare two restaurants, shops, or places you know well. Which is better and why?
What are you going to do this weekend? Are you excited? Why?
Describe your dream job. What would you do every day? Why is it perfect for you?

B1.name: Intermediate
B1.description: Questions encouraging opinions, reasons, and discussion of familiar abstract topics. Use of conditionals, present perfect, and modal verbs is expected.

questions.question: How has technology changed the way we communicate with friends and family? Is this change positive or negative?
What are the main advantages and disadvantages of social media in our daily lives?
Should every student be required to learn a foreign language at school? Why or why not?
How important is it to follow a healthy lifestyle? What does a healthy lifestyle include?
What kind of music do you think influences young people the most, and why?
Do you think people are happier today than they were 50 years ago? Give reasons for your answer.
What is the best way to learn a new language? Share your experience and advice.
How important is travel for personal growth? Can people grow without ever leaving their country?
Should children have access to smartphones? At what age, and with what restrictions?
What does success mean to you? Has your definition changed over the years?
How can ordinary people protect the environment in their daily lives? Which actions matter most?
Is it better to work for a big company or to be self-employed? What are the trade-offs?
What role does sport play in society? Is it just entertainment, or something more?
How important is family compared to friends in different stages of life?
Do you think reading books is still important in the digital age? Why or why not?
Should fast food be banned or limited in schools? What should be served instead?
What are the pros and cons of working from home? Would you prefer it long-term?
How has your country changed in the last ten years? Are you optimistic about its future?
Is it important to know the history of your country? What can history teach us today?
What can governments and citizens do to reduce pollution in big cities?
Should people retire earlier or later than they do today? What are the consequences either way?
How important are art, music, and physical education in schools? Should they be optional?
What qualities make a good leader, whether in politics, business, or daily life?
Do celebrities have too much influence on young people? Should this influence be limited?
Should we trust news that we see on social media? How can we know what is real?
How do you deal with stress in your life? What advice would you give to a stressed friend?
Is online shopping better than going to physical stores? What are you losing or gaining?
What is the most useful invention of the last 100 years? Defend your choice.
Should public transport be free for everyone? What problems would this solve or create?
What are the most important qualities in a friend? Has this changed since you were a child?

B2.name: Upper-Intermediate
B2.description: Questions requiring nuanced argumentation, comparison of perspectives, and discussion of abstract or socially complex issues. Use of complex grammar including conditionals, passive voice, and hypothetical structures is expected.

questions.question: To what extent should governments regulate social media platforms, and where should the line be drawn between safety and free expression?
Is the gig economy ultimately beneficial or harmful to workers? Consider flexibility, security, and long-term consequences.
Should higher education be free for everyone, or does free education devalue the experience and burden taxpayers unfairly?
How do you think artificial intelligence will reshape the job market in the next two decades? Who will benefit and who will suffer?
Is cancel culture a healthy form of social accountability or a serious threat to open dialogue?
When countries face a choice between economic growth and environmental protection, which should take priority and why?
To what extent are we responsible for the historical actions of our nation or ancestors? Should we apologize for things we did not do?
Is it ethical to use animals for scientific and medical research? Are there alternatives we should be pursuing?
Should voting be compulsory in democratic countries? What does democracy lose if many citizens choose not to participate?
How can we balance individual freedom with collective responsibility during a public health crisis?
Are exams an effective way to measure a student's abilities, or do they reward the wrong skills?
Do wealthy nations have a moral obligation to help poorer ones? If so, in what form, and with what conditions?
Is remote work a long-term shift in how humans organize labor, or a temporary trend that will fade?
To what extent does the media shape public opinion rather than reflect it? Can a truly informed public exist today?
Should genetically modified food be widely accepted, banned, or strictly regulated? What evidence informs your view?
Is privacy a luxury we can no longer afford in the digital age, or a fundamental right we must defend at all costs?
Should governments offer a universal basic income to all citizens? What would change in society if they did?
How important is it to preserve endangered languages and dying cultural traditions? Who should pay the cost of preservation?
Are zoos justifiable in modern society, or are they outdated institutions we should phase out?
Should there be limits to free speech online? Who should decide where those limits lie?
Is it possible to live a fully ethical life within a capitalist economy, or is some complicity unavoidable?
How should societies care for an aging population without overburdening younger generations?
To what extent should parents monitor and control their children's online activity? When does protection become surveillance?
Is space exploration worth its enormous cost while serious problems remain unsolved on Earth?
Should the use of fossil fuels be banned within the next twenty years? What are the realistic alternatives?
Are international sporting events like the Olympics still relevant, or have they become commercial spectacles disconnected from their ideals?
Should fashion brands be held legally responsible for the environmental damage caused by fast fashion?
Does globalization benefit everyone equally, or does it concentrate wealth and power while spreading risk?
Should we be concerned about declining birth rates in many developed countries? Why are people having fewer children?
Is it ever justifiable to break the law for a moral reason? Where would you draw the line?

C1.name: Advanced
C1.description: Questions demanding sophisticated argumentation, recognition of multiple perspectives, and engagement with abstract, philosophical, or specialized concepts. Lexical precision, idiomatic flexibility, and structural variety are expected.

questions.question: Critically evaluate the claim that meritocracy is a myth used to legitimize structural inequality rather than a system that genuinely rewards talent and effort.
To what extent does technological progress correlate with moral progress? Can a society become more advanced without becoming more humane?
Is the concept of national identity becoming obsolete in a globalized world, or is it experiencing a meaningful resurgence in response to globalization?
Should artistic expression have limits when it causes genuine offense to certain communities? Who has the authority to set those limits?
Discuss the ethical implications of editing the human genome. Where should we draw the line between curing disease and engineering enhancement?
Can democracy survive in an age dominated by algorithmic content curation, micro-targeted advertising, and large-scale disinformation?
To what extent is our perception of reality shaped by the language we speak? Could speakers of radically different languages live in fundamentally different worlds?
Is the pursuit of happiness a meaningful life goal, or is it a culturally constructed illusion that may be making us less content?
Should historical figures be judged by the moral standards of their own time or those of our own? What is at stake in either choice?
Discuss the inevitable tension between individual privacy and collective security. Can we have both, or must one always be sacrificed for the other?
Is consumerism inherently incompatible with environmental sustainability, or can a 'green capitalism' deliver on its promises?
To what extent does art have a moral responsibility to its audience and society, and to what extent must it remain free of such obligations?
Can true objectivity exist in journalism, or is bias an inevitable feature of any human attempt to describe reality?
Is the notion of free will compatible with what modern neuroscience tells us about the brain? What hangs on the answer?
Should governments and courts be allowed to use artificial intelligence in judicial decisions such as sentencing or parole?
To what extent is widespread loneliness a symptom of contemporary society rather than a private, individual condition?
Is the canonical Western literary tradition still relevant in a multicultural, postcolonial world, or does its centrality crowd out other voices?
Discuss the proposition that some degree of suffering is necessary, or even essential, for genuine personal growth.
To what extent should cultural traditions be preserved when they conflict with internationally recognized human rights?
Is education primarily a public good that benefits society as a whole, or a private investment whose costs and rewards belong to the individual?
Can a society function ethically without religion, or do shared transcendent narratives play a role that secular alternatives cannot replace?
To what extent does economic inequality undermine democratic institutions, even when formal political rights remain intact?
Should humanity colonize other planets, given our environmental track record on Earth? Would we be exporting hope or our worst tendencies?
Is artistic genius primarily the product of innate talent or of sociocultural circumstances that allow certain individuals to flourish?
Discuss the paradox of tolerance: how should an open society respond to those who would dismantle its openness?
To what extent does the language we inherit constrain how we are able to think, and to what extent can thought escape language altogether?
Should companies be held legally accountable for the deliberately addictive design of their products, from social media to mobile games?
Is nostalgia a constructive emotional response to a complex present, or a barrier that prevents us from engaging meaningfully with the future?
Can violence ever be morally justified in the pursuit of justice, and if so, what conditions would have to be met?
Are human beings fundamentally cooperative or fundamentally competitive? What does the answer imply for how we design our institutions?

C2.name: Proficiency
C2.description: Questions designed for highly proficient speakers capable of engaging with abstract, philosophical, and specialized discourse. Demands precision of expression, command of register, and the ability to develop, qualify, and defend complex theses.

questions.question: Reconcile the apparent contradiction between hard determinism and the lived phenomenology of moral agency. Can responsibility survive without metaphysical freedom?
Adjudicate between communitarian and liberal-individualist conceptions of justice in light of contemporary global crises such as climate change and mass migration.
Is the Anthropocene a meaningful epistemic category, or a self-aggrandizing narrative through which humans inflate their geological significance?
To what extent do epistemic frameworks borrowed from the natural sciences inadequately capture the irreducible complexity of human social phenomena?
Critique the proposition that beauty is a culturally contingent construct rather than the apprehension of objective form. What is at stake on either side?
Can a coherent ethics of care be derived from evolutionary biology, or must it ultimately be grounded in transcendent principles that biology cannot provide?
Examine the contention that consciousness is fundamentally irreducible to physical processes, and assess the implications for the possibility of artificial minds.
Defend or refute the proposition that history possesses an underlying telos, despite the apparent contingency of historical events.
To what extent does the commodification of attention in the digital economy undermine the very possibility of authentic selfhood?
Interrogate the assumption that progress in scientific knowledge necessarily entails ethical or civilizational advancement.
Critically assess whether postmodern skepticism toward grand narratives has itself congealed into a grand narrative that demands its own deconstruction.
Is the demand for representational justice in the arts compatible with the autonomy traditionally afforded to artistic expression, or are the two ultimately at war?
Examine the philosophical foundations of human rights, considering whether they retain normative force absent any metaphysical grounding.
To what extent does the proliferation of identity categories enable, rather than dismantle, the structures of social control they ostensibly challenge?
Reconcile, or sharply distinguish, the imperatives of restorative and retributive justice in cases of large-scale historical injustice.
Discuss whether the experience of the sublime remains genuinely accessible in an era of mediated, algorithmically curated stimuli.
Is the concept of authenticity philosophically coherent, or merely a romanticist residue that contemporary thought ought to outgrow?
To what extent does economic globalization presuppose, rather than threaten, the persistence of robust forms of cultural particularity?
Examine the proposition that liberal democracy contains the conditions of its own dissolution, and that its current crises are not anomalies but logical extensions of its premises.
Can the ethical demands of veganism be sustained without recourse to a sentimental anthropomorphism that arguably obscures the very animals it purports to defend?
To what extent is the modern preoccupation with mental health a symptom of, rather than a remedy for, the social pathologies it diagnoses?
Critically engage with the claim that language does not merely describe reality but constitutes it. What would the world look like if this were true, or false?
Is intergenerational justice possible within a political system structured almost entirely around the preferences of the living?
Examine whether the pursuit of radical life extension and digital immortality represents the apotheosis of human aspiration or its grotesque distortion.
To what extent has the discourse of 'sustainability' been co-opted to perpetuate the very systems it ostensibly seeks to transform?
Defend or critique the proposition that solitude is a precondition for genuine thought, in opposition to dialogical conceptions of reason as essentially intersubjective.
Is the notion of 'neutral' technology coherent, or are technical artifacts always already inscribed with the values, interests, and blind spots of their creators?
Reconcile the demand for radical inclusivity with the inevitability of normative judgment in any community capable of sustaining itself over time.
Examine whether forgiveness, properly understood, requires the recognition of wrongdoing on the part of the offender, or whether it must transcend such recognition entirely.
To what extent is the contemporary epistemic crisis fundamentally a problem of information abundance rather than scarcity, and what does this imply for the future of public reason?
